title: On Flow-based Generative Models for Probabilistic Forecasting

reviews.summary: The paper develops a principled connection between flow-based generative models (FBGMs) and discrete-time autoregressive probabilistic forecasting. Starting from linear SDE–based stochastic interpolants, the authors show that the Markovian-projection machinery underlying FBGMs can be recast as a constrained mean-field variational inference (CMFVI) problem for conditional exponential families, where the Bayes estimator of natural parameters yields closed-form conditional Gaussians. This produces a family of models—an MSE-trained non-autoregressive forecaster (q^MSE), an autoregressive Gaussian forecaster (q^MSE-AR), and a continuous-time neural latent SDE (q^Neural-SDE)—that share convenient theoretical properties with FBGMs while being simpler to train and use for time series. Experiments on synthetic dynamical systems compare the proposed models to flow-matching and conditional Gaussian baselines.
strengths: Connects SDE-based stochastic interpolation, Gaussian CRFs/message passing, and FBGM Markovian projection through a unifying CMFVI lens; the discrete-time “Markovian projection” interpretation for time series is conceptually neat.

The identification of the learned drift (or transition) as a Bayes estimator of a backward message (natural parameter) in a conditional exponential family clarifies how MSE-style training aligns with FBGM matching objectives.

The use of conditioned linear SDEs and their equivalence to chain-structured Gaussian CRFs provides a tractable bridge from continuous- to discrete-time models with efficient inference via message passing.
Implements multiple variants (q^MSE, q^MSE-AR, Neural SDE) and compares latents vs. observation-space training, reflecting awareness of modeling choices in practice.
Evaluates on several classical nonlinear dynamical systems and reports multiple metrics (likelihood-like and error-based).
Theoretical development is structured around clearly stated propositions and corollaries with references to appendices for proofs and implementation details.
Intuitive narratives (e.g., backward messages as learnable targets; “discrete-time Markovian projection”) help demystify the construction.

Offers a plausible route to marrying the interpretability and controllability of linear SDE/CRF constructions with the learning convenience of MSE objectives for time series, potentially reducing the practical friction of fully continuous-time neural SDEs.
weaknesses: The core CMFVI solution yields conditionally Gaussian models whose covariances are fixed with respect to observations (heteroscedasticity and data-dependent uncertainty are not captured), which limits probabilistic expressiveness and calibration.

The step from the continuous-time Markovian projection to the discrete-time autoregressive counterpart drops the dependence on the current state x_t in the Bayes estimator; the conditions under which this is a good approximation are not characterized.

The CMFVI objective essentially reduces to a plug-in Bayes estimator for natural parameters; novelty over classic empirical Bayes/plug-in estimators and prior FBGM matching interpretations is limited.
Validation is restricted to synthetic dynamical systems; no real-world multivariate benchmarks (e.g., Electricity, Exchange, Traffic, Weather) or irregularly sampled datasets are included.

Strong recent baselines in autoregressive flow/diffusion forecasting are missing (e.g., Recurrent Interpolants, Flow TIME, Conditionally Whitened FM/Diffusion, CSDI for conditional generation); comparisons are therefore insufficient to substantiate practical advantages.

The negative log-likelihood reporting is unclear (values can be negative), and the “heuristic” handling of the first-step distribution may bias results. Calibration metrics (e.g., CRPS, reliability) are absent.

Several typos/formatting artifacts, some incomplete captions, and “CITE” placeholders remain. A few key explanations (e.g., empirical NLL computation, sampling details for p(x|y_O) when covariances are fixed) are under-specified in the main text.

Important contemporaneous works on autoregressive FM/CFM and stepwise diffusion with learned priors (e.g., RI, Flow TIME, StochDiff, CW-Gen) and strong deep probabilistic baselines (e.g., DeepAR/DeepState, GluonTS ecosystem) are not compared empirically; coverage in the related work is partial.
reviews.questions:
1. How exactly is the “negative log-likelihood” computed in Table 1? If the model is Gaussian AR with closed-form conditionals, why are some NLL values negative, and what normalization (per step/dimension) is used?
2. Your main results assume potential covariances are fixed w.r.t. observations. Can the CMFVI derivation be extended to learn observation-dependent covariances (e.g., via an additional Bayes estimator), and if so, what breaks in the message-passing or complexity?
3. In Proposition 7 the discrete-time model replaces β_t^*(x_t, …) with β_t^*(x_{1:k−1}, …). When does this approximation fail empirically (e.g., chaotic regimes, long horizons)? Can you provide an error bound or an empirical ablation varying step size/discretization?
4. For the “first-step” distribution you use a heuristic sample from the stochastic interpolant conditioned only on y_O. How sensitive are results to this choice, and why not learn p(x_{t_{k+1}} | y_O) directly?
5. Could you report calibration metrics (CRPS/CRPS-sum, reliability diagrams) and include comparisons on at least two standard real datasets (e.g., Electricity, Traffic, Exchange, Weather) to substantiate practical relevance?
6. How does training/inference cost compare to your FBGM baseline and to a strong AR flow/diffusion baseline (e.g., Flow TIME or RI)? Please include wall-clock time, solver steps, and FLOPs if possible.
7. Are there cases where q^MSE-AR materially outperforms q^Neural-SDE in both accuracy and speed, validating the claim that the discrete-time surrogate is preferable in practice?
reviews.assessment: This paper presents a thoughtful and technically coherent perspective that unifies flow-based generative modeling, conditioned linear SDEs/CRFs, and MSE training via a constrained mean-field variational view. The discrete-time “Markovian projection” interpretation is appealing and could help demystify how to port continuous-time flow ideas to practical autoregressive forecasters. However, the instantiated models are ultimately conditional Gaussians with observation-independent covariances—too restrictive for many probabilistic forecasting tasks—and the empirical evidence is limited (synthetic-only, mixed outcomes, unclear likelihood reporting) and omits strong contemporary baselines. I view the conceptual contribution as promising but the current experimental validation and modeling scope as insufficient for NeurIPS. Strengthening the empirical section (real-world datasets, calibration metrics, runtime), relaxing the fixed-covariance assumption, and comparing to recent autoregressive flow/diffusion methods would significantly improve the paper’s impact and persuasiveness.
